data.title: My 10 REIT Portfolio That Pays Me $3,000 Each Month
data.description:
Real estate can be a great investment to earn passive income.

All you have to do is collect your monthly rent check, and that's it.

That's at least the perception online.

But the reality is very different, and if you have ever owned rental properties, you can attest to that.

Each new property can require 100, 200, or even more hours of work, even before the property is rented out and generating cash flow.

You have to first educate yourself on your local market. Then you need to meet brokers. Visit lots of different properties. Do your due diligence. Finally, make offers. Negotiate. Close a deal. Secure financing. Do renovations. Market the property. Interview tenants. Do background checks. Have lawyers draft rental agreements. Collect a deposit. Etc.

The work is much more significant than people realize.

And it does not end there.

Once you have your tenant in place, you will still need to deal with maintenance issues, occasional tenant disputes, repairs, insurance claims, mortgage payments, vacancies, potential evictions, etc.

The work never ends. This is not true passive income.

Luckily, there is a better way to invest in rental properties, and that's through REITs.

REITs stand for real estate investment trusts. Lots of them are publicly listed and allow you to invest in rental properties in the same way you would invest in any other sector - by simply buying shares.

Best of all, most REITs are professionally managed and own very large real estate portfolios, often in the billions, resulting in substantial economies of scale. According to some studies, the average management cost of REITs is only 50 basis points of assets per year, or about 2-3x lower than the management cost of private properties:

As such, REITs combine the best of both worlds: stocks and real estate, and allow investors to invest in real estate without its biggest downsides.

They are professionally managed, cost-efficient, and generate significant true passive income.

I just added it up and found out that I am currently earning about $3,000 per month on average from a roughly $690,000 investment in the following 10 REIT investments:

Vesta REIT (VTMX) is only offering a low 2.6% dividend yield, but it still generates me a lot of income because I have invested heavily in it, and my yield on cost is closer to 4%. Even then, the yield is not particularly high, but that is only because the REIT retains about 2/3 of its cash flow to reinvest in growth. Vesta is the leading industrial REIT in Mexico, and it enjoys great opportunities to develop new properties at initial yields in the double digits as it capitalizes on the growing trend of nearshoring. This creates a lot of value for shareholders and is expected to lead to 10% annual FFO and dividend per share growth through 2030. As such, its yield could reach closer to 5% in the not-so-distant future. I expect patience to be richly rewarded.

Vonovia (VNA/VONOY) is the leading apartment landlord in Europe. Its dividend yield is about 5.5% at the current share price, and that’s with a low 55% payout ratio. The company is massive in size, owning 500,000+ apartment units, primarily in Germany, where rents are regulated in a way that results in highly consistent and predictable 3-4% growth each year. The growth continued even during the Great Financial Crisis and the pandemic. As such, its assets are much safer and less cyclical than comparable apartment communities in the US, and this explains why these assets are very popular in the private market and generally trade at ultra-low cap rates in the high 3s or low 4s. But because listed real estate companies are today out of favor, Vonovia is currently offered at a huge 55% discount to NAV and an 11% FFO yield. I think that it is presenting a historic opportunity to buy a stake in German apartment communities at a huge discount.

Crown Castle (CCI) is the highest-yielding cell tower REIT, currently offering a 5.3% dividend yield, which is far more than what its close peers American Tower (AMT) and SBA Communications (SBAC) are offering. Generally, a higher yield implies that the REIT owns weaker and riskier assets, but this is not the case here. CCI is the only cell tower REIT to focus exclusively on the US market, which is the safest market with the highest barriers to entry in the world. I think that the higher yield is largely the result of the REIT’s recent transformation, which has been overlooked by the market. CCI used to trade at a steep discount due to its fiber assets and higher leverage, which weren’t popular, but it has since then gotten rid of them and paid off a bunch of debt. Over time, as the market recognises this, I expect the stock to recover to higher levels.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) just recently slashed its dividend by 40%, and yet, even despite that, it still remains one of my biggest dividend payers. That’s because I unfortunately owned quite a bit of it prior to the cut and have bought more of it since then. Following its crash, the valuation is now the lowest ever for the company, and even with a low 40% payout ratio, its dividend yield is still 6.2%. The life science property sector is severely oversupplied due to the combination of too much speculative development following the pandemic and demand destruction caused by the policy chaos of the current administration. This is a big setback, but I still remain optimistic about the long term. The AI revolution is expected to unleash a golden age in healthcare innovation, making these properties more productive and valuable than ever before to their end users. It is also breaking barriers to entry, and I expect it to lead to an exploration in new biotech formation over the coming years, which will then also increase the competition for these assets. The oversupply is so significant that the recovery will likely take 5+ years, but I think that the wait is worthwhile given how much upside potential it could present in that scenario.

HA Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) is not officially structured as a REIT anymore, but I still included it in this list because it used to be one and still operates a mortgage REIT-like business model, lending capital to renewable energy projects. The company is today offering a 4.7% dividend yield, and importantly, it has guided to grow its FFO per share by 8-10% annually through 2030. This should also lead to rapidly rising dividend income and upside to shareholders.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA / CNNRF) is offering a near 6% dividend yield with a low 55% payout ratio. That’s one of the highest yields of any net lease REITs and one of the lowest payout ratios as well. Its peers like Realty Income (O) offer a lower yield at closer to 5% with a higher payout ratio in the 70s. What makes Canadian Net so much more rewarding? It simply trades at a lower valuation at just 10x FFO. It is the only net lease REIT that exists in Canada, and it has been overlooked by investors. Its dividend has grown by 8% per year on average over the past decade, which is about 2x faster than the growth of its lower-yielding American peers.

Big Yellow Group (BYG/BYLOF) is my largest self-storage investment, and yielding 5.6%, it is resulting in significant dividend income. I have been accumulating it since the Brexit crash of 2016, and all the purchases add up. The story here is simple: the European self-storage market is structurally undersupplied with 20x less storage per capita than in the US, but the concept is now growing in popularity, resulting in lots of high-yielding development opportunities for these REITs. American self-storage REITs went through a similar period of heavy development activity 10-20 years ago, and this period was extremely rewarding for them, resulting in 15-20% annual returns. Big Yellow is now replicating the same model in Europe, and we expect similar growth and upside over the coming decade.

NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) is a smaller position for me, but it still contributes lots of dividend income, given that its yield is 11.5%! It is the highest yielder in my entire portfolio. Typically, such high yields indicate that the REIT is overleveraged and paying an unsustainable dividend, but this is not the case here. The REIT has no debt, and its dividend is well covered with an 83% payout ratio. Rather, the high yield is the result of the REIT’s low valuation. It specializes in cannabis cultivation facilities, which are out of favor today. Many tenants are facing financial difficulties, and lease defaults are frequent in this sector. The good news is that NLCP has done a great job at mitigating risks by focusing on properties in limited-license states with long triple-net leases with above-average operators. The REIT has managed to steadily grow its dividend since going public in 2021, and with the current administration preparing to soon reschedule cannabis as a less dangerous Schedule III drug, the whole sector should soon get a nice boost. We think that the dividend is sustainable.

Sila Realty Trust (SILA) is my biggest dividend investment in the healthcare property sector. The REIT is today offering a 6.9% dividend yield that’s well covered with a safe 70% payout ratio and backed by an underleveraged balance sheet with a 4x debt-to-EBITDA. The REIT owns primarily medical office buildings, surgical facilities, and rehabilitation centers, all under long-term triple net leases with steady annual rent escalations. These properties greatly benefit from the rapidly aging population, resulting in growing recession-resistant demand. The high yield, steady growth, and reasonable valuation make it a great long-term pick for dividend investors in my opinions

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA/BSRTF) is my biggest residential REIT investment, generating me significant passive income. It pays a 4.7% dividend yield, and what’s unique about BSR is that it pays on a monthly basis and often classifies the dividend as return of capital, making it far more tax efficient. I also like that the REIT focuses on affordable apartment communities of the rapidly growing Texas Triangle, resulting in strong long-term rent growth potential. Even then, it is today discounted because these markets have faced some oversupply in recent years, which has hurt fundamentals. I think that this is only temporary and expect an acceleration in rent growth going into 2027 as new supply deliveries will drop back to historically low levels. I expect this to improve the market sentiment and lead to significant upside, given that the REIT is currently trading at a huge 35% discount to its net asset value. I earn significant monthly dividend income while I wait.

Closing Note

These are the exact type of REIT investments that I like to target, as they generate significant and growing passive income.

These are others, of course, as well. When including Foreign REITs, I own close to 50 of them from a universe of 1,000+ opportunities.

As such, our real estate portfolio is very well diversified at High Yield Landlord, and even despite occasionally suffering setbacks, like in the case of Alexandria, we are still able to grow our passive monthly income each year and have so far also beaten our sector benchmarks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vistra Is Capitalizing On America's Energy Boom; Growth Will Improve Valuation Sooner Or Later
data.description:
I have covered Vistra Corp. (VST) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it an interesting buy. Since then, the company reported record 2025 figures and confirmed its 2026 guidance.

Background

Vistra Corp. is a U.S. power player combining a sizable retail electricity business with a diversified generation fleet spread across 18 states and serving close to 5 million customers under several retail brands. The company operates one of the largest generation fleets in the country, with around 44 GW of capacity distributed across different generation facilities, mainly natural gas, but it also has an important presence in coal and nuclear plants. Renewables are residual with 3% of their capacity.

While Vistra's nuclear plants only represent 15% of its capacity, they generated more electricity than its coal plants, with 24% of total production in 2025, highlighting the importance of the Energy Harbor acquisition in 2024. This also implies that the company could greatly benefit from an increase in power demand, as its coal and gas assets are operating between 50% and 60% of their capacity.

In fact, Vistra management expects that 2026 and 2027 will extend this growth, and this trend could be extended even longer as they expect data center demand not to tighten supply-demand until late 2027 or early 2028.

Our primary regions continue to outperform. We maintain our view that annual peak load growth of at least 3% to 5% in ERCOT and low single-digit growth in PJM is achievable through 2030. Importantly, we expect overall load growth to outpace peak demand, resulting in higher expected utilization across the system rather than short-duration peaks alone, implying the economics of existing generation assets will improve on a sustained basis.

As explained in the previous article, most of Vistra's growth has come from acquisitions, and I expect this trend to continue after it integrates Cogentrix Energy’s 10 natural gas plants. However, Vistra is not relying only on M&A; the company is also building new gas plants and doing conversions. This, together with expected demand growth, I expect will propel the company’s revenue over the next few years.

Regarding the capital allocation, the company has slightly improved its projections. Vistra has increased its expected cumulative cash to allocate through YE 2027 from around $10 billion to more than $10 billion. However, it has increased the funds invested for growth to $4 billion (from $2.6 billion) and has reduced shareholder returns from $3.4 billion to $3 billion. Despite this “announced” reduction, the current repurchase pace implies that around $1.5 billion will be invested in repurchases in 2026, and the company will maintain its target of around $300 million in dividends annually.

Financial Position and Valuation

Vistra has a comfortable financial position with $785 million in cash and $19 billion in debt. Its leverage ratio stands at 2.6x, in line with its target to be below 3x. This means that the company is still in a position to explore some acquisitions. Moreover, most of its debt matures beyond 2030, providing plenty of flexibility in the short term, as it only has a $500 million maturity in 2026.

Vistra delivered good Q4 results and reached a record year. The company also confirmed its 2026 guidance with an EBITDA between $6.8 and $7.6 billion. These numbers are backed by a very high hedging level, and all of the 2026 generation is already hedged or under PPAs, with a large portion of the 2027 results covered as well. Management also reiterated its 2027 EBITDA midpoint opportunity between $7.4 and $7.8 billion, before any upside from Cogentrix.

According to management, this can translate into an almost 60% increase in the cash generation per share by 2027 compared to 2025.

Assuming that the company spends $2 billion of the uncommitted cash to reduce debt and maintains the current 12x EV/EBITDA multiple, it will situate my fair value at a $214 stock price, representing about a 40% upside.

This is assuming the company achieves the midpoint of 2027 guidance; however, it wouldn’t surprise me if the company exceeds this and achieves the higher end, as it did in 2025.

Risks

Vistra’s main risk is the commodity and electricity price volatility; however, Vistra hedges most of it, and it has hedges or PPAs for most of the next two years. Despite that, even when hedges roll off, I expect electricity prices to stay elevated due to all the AI consumption.

Credit and refinancing are other risks. While debt is at normal levels for this type of company and Vistra does not have important near-term maturities, a prolonged period of very tight credit or higher interest rates would eat into free cash flow and potentially force management to slow buybacks or growth.

Moreover, there is always execution and integration risk with big deals like Energy Harbor and Cogentrix.

Finally, for me, the biggest risk right now is not company-specific but is related to a possible market correction due to the Iran war. If there is a strong market correction, which I believe is highly possible, the stock will move down regardless of being mostly insulated by its hedges. This is the main reason I don’t upgrade the stock to a strong buy.

Conclusion

Vistra Corp confirmed its guidance and improved its cash generation through 2027. I expect the company to continue growing and, in case of any market meltdown, to take the opportunity to acquire some company (if credit markets remain open), as its leverage remains below the company’s target.

With expected EBITDA and cash flow growth, I moved the fair value to $214 per share, representing more than a 40% upside.

data.title: Cheesecake Factory: Too Premium For Value, Too Casual For Premium
data.description:
I was scrolling through my phone the other day and discovered that Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) has a high-calorie Oreo slice with ~1,700 calories.

Useless trivia, I know. Calories per dollar wouldn't make sense here.

But I just keep thinking about how many hours and hours of lifting I'd need to burn all that off.

Oh, I almost forgot I'm always bulking!

Speaking of gaining weight, this stock didn't bulk up post-earnings. And don't get me wrong, this wasn't a bad quarter given the American consumer's wallet in Q4.

Probably the slip-up I mentioned in the last article—competing with fast-casuals and not other casual dining—is already impacting same-store sales. I'll tell you about that soon.

First, I want to give you a peek at my 'Hold' rating here.

Looks like it aged well, don't you think?

I had flagged the stock as fairly valued just above $60.

It even touched $65, but quickly fell back into the $50s after earnings, boosted by Cheesecake Factory and perhaps offset by Flower Child and some other FRC concepts.

As the spin-off seems to be off the table (I even discussed this optionality in another article), I'll keep viewing the stock as a casual dining option with top-notch AUVs, but snail-paced organic growth (at least in the core concept), and Capex-heavy.

But you know what?

The million-bowl question (piggybacking on this new strategy) is whether The Cheesecake Factory sits in that group or ends up like First Watch (FWRG), holding off on value until the last minute.

That's the dilemma of the more 'premium-like' casual dining chains.

Well, BJ's (BJRI) did very well with a prix fixe lunch menu. And if they could do it, why couldn't The Cheesecake Factory?

The ‘Bowl Effect’

Don't tell me you imagined Cheesecake Factory would report strong same-store sales? To me, it smelled strongly of something bad coming. At the very least, bland.

Remember that chains that implemented prix fixe promotions ended up bringing guests back (especially for lunches with an average check of up to ~$15 per guest).

This was the case for Applebee's (DIN), BJ's Restaurants, and especially Chili's (EAT).

But do you know who else suffered from the traffic problem?

You're right if you thought of upscale casual dining chains.

Thinking aloud, I imagine these restaurants fell somewhere between too expensive for a weekday family lunch and too 'normal' for a fancy meal with your wife. And, since Cheesecake Factory has all of that, they ended up being swallowed up.

How could I forget! The bowl strategy came in to close that gap.

Slightly lower price points (bowls are priced between $15.00 and $20.00 against the average Cheesecake Factory check of ~$31.05) and an appeal to GLP-1 users was management's idea to close that gap.

The average check for each customer, including beverages and desserts, was approximately $31.05 during fiscal 2024.

I told you in the last article that they were 'fighting the wrong enemy'.

I don't know about you, but I pictured them in WWE, throwing punches at the wrong guy. And this 'wrong opponent' is fast-casuals like Chipotle (CMG) and Pollo Loco (LOCO), for example.

You hit the nail on the head if you said that this could dilute the brand.

But there's something that almost no one talks about, and it has to do with the illusion of traffic.

Even if the bowls brought in some extra traffic (which I'm not really convinced of since traffic was down 1.7% to 2.2% in Q4), the more they show up in the mix, the more they pull down the average check.

The idea here seems to be to attract guests who wouldn't visit them for weekday lunch, for example. Core customers come about once a quarter and order dishes with a higher average check.

So, everything that comes in should be incremental.

That's not what I think Cheesecake Factory guests would be like, anyway. These folks want convenience, and that's exactly what fast-casuals offer for at least $5 less.

And that's without considering the trade-down to other chains.

If you're going to eat at a sit-down restaurant, options like Applebee's '2 for $25' or Chili's '3 For Me' are probably more appealing.

It feels more like a proper FSR lunch, just at a lower price.

Kind of feels like they're counting on add-ons to lift the average check. Maybe alcohol and, most likely, dessert. Something that health-conscious guests wouldn't touch.

If you ask me, they should focus more on some fixed-value platform targeting different guests.

Probably some bundle meal with an appetizer, main course, and dessert for ~$20 or $25 (then you could put in a more chicken-heavy mix so as not to pressure margins) would be something that would bring more sustainable traffic without hurting the average check too much.

Using desserts seems to be the key here. It's their differentiator.

Here's a peek at same-store sales:

Notice that both The Cheesecake Factory and North Italia are underperforming most casual dining establishments out there. Even though the CEO said they outperformed BBI.

But as I told you, the 'Bowl Effect' appears when I break that down.

The CEO said the price increased by ~3.5 to 4% while the mix went negative by 1.8%. From that, I can deduce that traffic fell between 1.7 and 2.2%, as I told you before.

I'll now turn to sales trends. Industry sales decelerated in the fourth quarter as reflected by the Black Box casual dining index declining sequentially by 410 basis points from the third quarter. The Cheesecake Factory's comparable sales were negative 2.2% in the fourth quarter, down from 0.3% in the third quarter, demonstrating relative stability in comparison to the industry sequential decline.

Now, there's no traffic compensating for that mix.

From what I understand, increasing their traditional pricing would kind of subsidize these bowls (which are dilutive for the mix, as you can see).

There could be more pressure ahead if they stick with this strategy.

After all, the value wars in casual dining have been going on for a while, and there aren't many value levers in the Cheesecake Factory menu besides the 'Bites & Bowls'.

North Italia is also under pressure, I've noticed a few reasons for this. Slightly different, but ultimately it's all due to the trade-down from more premium-like chains to more accessible casual dining.

I want you to start by taking a look at same-store sales for North Italia, comparing them to some peers:

Unlike the Cheesecake Factory, North Italia even runs a value menu called the '$25 Lunch Special' (an appetizer and a main course for $25). On the other hand, Maggiano's is trying a more Texas Roadhouse-like (TXRH) approach, increasing portion sizes by approximately 20%.

Both are underperforming cheaper chains like Olive Garden (DRI) (before and after the Never Ending Pasta Bowl) which is betting on $15 BOGO promotions and smaller portions starting at $13.

Not much mystery here.

Value war has made its way into Italian dining too, and it should keep putting pressure on chains like North Italia as long as folks are looking to spend less when eating out.

Margins, Closures, and Everything Else

As most of their key concepts (apart from some smaller ones in FRC) aren't meat-heavy, pricing usually does a good job offsetting low single-digit inflation.

Dairy’s been coming down, which definitely helps—especially for cakes and cheese.

For Cheesecake Factory, restaurant-level margins were up around 60 bp, landing at 17.6%. Even with a payback period of approximately 4 to 5 years, they maintained AUVs of approximately $12 million.

But they're still closing some restaurants.

Want to know why? I'm not sure, but I have some clues.

One of those Cheesecake Factory locations (Baltimore Inner Harbor, MD) is near at least 10 other full-service restaurants in the vicinity.

It's that old competition problem I mentioned before.

This is also a high-end area, and I imagine the rent must be very expensive.

And, as the model relies on operational leverage (giant stores to obtain giant AUVs), the slow traffic may have been the final straw that led to its closure.

Friendship Heights in Washington, DC looks like a bit of a different situation.

The store has been around for over 30 years, and there aren't as many restaurants nearby as in Baltimore, except for a Maggiano's that has also been operating for decades.

Both spots come with expensive leases. But I'd guess this one got a bit more affordable with federal spending cuts, which has always been a big driver of spending in DC.

The other two closures were in the same property, Galleria, the largest shopping mall in Texas.

In those cases, I imagine it has more to do with the concepts themselves than with anything operational. FRC's 'dual-branded' restaurants (Bianco Cocina and Cantina) probably have low AUVs and management decided to cut them to focus on Flower Child/Culinary Dropout, and the Grand Lux ​​Cafe is simply more outdated.

As they expect 26 net openings for FY 2026 (that's right, with no more closures from then on), they would maintain their ~7% footprint growth intact.

It doesn't seem to be a structural problem yet.

If same-store sales continue to fall for some time, then things would start to get worse. These cuts seem to have been to eliminate below-average AUVs with expensive leases.

Quite rational, to be honest.

Looking To FY 2026

I think management was quite conservative with their guidance, and for good reason. Even though RNA's RPI ticked up in January and February, I still imagine the trade-down is happening.

For sales, expect revenue of $3.9 billion. Basically, another 4% year-over-year increase. Wall St. is just a little more optimistic, pinpointing something close to $3.91 billion.

For me, this already incorporates same-store sales flat or growing in the low single digits and the contribution of new restaurants.

What changes is the bottom line.

For example, if I model an EBITDA margin similar to that of FY 2025 (8 to 9%), I get something close to $312 to $351 million. And really, I don't think it should go much higher than that.

Management also made it very clear that the net profit margin is 5%.

Well, if we assume the revenue they guided, that's $195 million ($3.93 EPS considering stable outstanding shares as they guided).

If you ask me, that's a little risky.

A net margin of 5% is just under 300 bp above the 5-year average, and in a scenario of more expensive energy, I imagine some inflationary pressures and operational deleveraging here and there.

As you saw, the closures were probably to prune the system and keep them with strong margins.

In my base scenario, I imagine a net margin of ~3.5 to 4.5% ($3.15 EPS at the midpoint). Practically flat compared to this year.

Plugging in an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion of approximately 80 to 90% (the range that has remained for the last three years), the midpoint EBITDA of $331.5 million would become an OCF of $265.2 to $298.3 million. Since the guided Capex was $210 million, the FCF would be in the range of $55.2 to $88.3 million.

See, this makes them capable, in most cases, of paying the $1.18 million in annual dividends that Wall Street expects (returning something close to $58.8 million) and still have some left over for share buybacks.

Still a 'Hold'?

Now that I have everything on the table, it's time to try and answer whether this stock is still in my 'Hold' box.

Starting with the simplest, if I take peers with company-owned restaurants like Brinker and BJ's Restaurants, the average P/E is close to 14 to 16x.

And, plugging in the EPS I modeled for The Cheesecake Factory, the price target is somewhere between $44 and $50.40. Looking at the guided EPS ($3.93) this increases to $55 to $62.90.

Taking a midpoint of both my range and the guided range, we have something close to ~$53 for FY 2026.

I also want to take a peek at other multiples.

For example, if I take my OCF projection of $281.7 million ($5.67 per share), I find that the stock is now trading at a P/CF of ~9.7x. It is higher than BJ's (6.8x) and Brinker (8.5x), and if I consider the 5-year average of Cheesecake itself (7.5x), the price target would be around $43 per share.

I don't think it's the OCF that will move the needle here.

The Cheesecake Factory's investment thesis is still about the incremental ROI they would get from opening FRC stores while subsidizing that with the core concept.

I'll take a peek at models that plug in growth.

If I imagine that EPS of $3.15 for FY 2026 growing to the Wall St. projection of $4.92 in FY 2028, that's a CAGR of ~16%. But if I imagine the $3.95 from management, it would decrease to something like ~8%.

I'll use both anyway. But with some differences.

A good base case here is to take the 10-year CAGR of the diluted EPS (~3%) and plug it into the guided EPS. This would give me a price target of ~$54 without the 25% safety margin.

There are some problems here, of course.

At that time they didn't have the FRC, and I probably need to normalize that for post-2019. It's something close to ~14% CAGR.

But assuming that would reduce to something around a mid-single digit (after all, we're aiming for the CAGR of the next ten years, and not looking back after the initial ramp-up).

The price target here, already assuming a 25% safety margin, is between $51 and $62. Something that the stock already seems to be pricing in.

To wrap this up, I'll imagine a base scenario for FY 2030.

Market expects ~$4.7 billion in sales, I'll take advantage of that.

For that, I'll imagine a more stable EBITDA margin close to 10% ($470 million EBITDA). Oh, I almost forgot. ~9x EBITDA seems safe for a mature casual dining chain at this point.

This would give me a price target of approximately $78.50. Discounting to present value at a cost of equity of 10.05% (risk-free rate of 4.34%, beta of 1.01 and expected return of ~10%), a discounted price target of ~$53.40.

Even if you expect higher multiples of ~10 to 11x, the upside would still be low at $59.70 and $66.20 per share.

We went around and around and came to the same conclusion (and, looking back, it seems we were right). Cheesecake Factory is perfectly priced in a base scenario.

In a bull scenario there's an upside of ~15 to 20%, but it's not a clear asymmetry here. Even more so when they're seeing a trade-down.

For me, it's still a 'Hold'.

data.title: Veeva Systems: Growth Has More Legs Now
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Veeva Systems (VEEV) as I expected revenue growth to accelerate given that VEEV saw strong adoption of its Vault CRM and also that the IQVIA dispute had been resolved. My view is more positive now (reiterate buy). VEEV’s growth strength is no longer limited to CRM migration alone. R&D and Quality are showing traction, Commercial is starting to pull through adjacent solutions, and AI looks more like a tailwind than a threat.

R&D and Quality

The biggest development is in R&D and Quality. In my earlier thesis, my view on R&D was mostly about strong demand in clinical, regulatory, and quality workflows. Clearly, things have progressed in the right direction, as the wins are spreading across more products, not just the core products. The evidence to back this is:

  1. A top 20 biopharma selected Veeva RTSM (Randomization and Trial Supply Management) as its enterprise standard; and
  2. Safety won its sixth top 20 biopharma customer in the quarter; and
  3. The first top 20 biopharma went live on Workbench and Signal; and
  4. Even in regulatory, the 19th of the top 20 biopharma selected Submissions as part of a multi-year transformation deal; and
  5. As for quality, VEEV added 20 QualityDocs customers and 27 QMS (Quality Management System) customers in the quarter, including a top 20 biopharma that selected QMS as its global standard. This led to VEEV ending the year with 450 QMS customers, including 15 of the top 20 biopharmas.
  6. Finally, LIMS (Laboratory Information Management System) also saw its first top 20 early adopter on track to have two manufacturing sites live later this year.

While we don’t know the exact revenue/profit contribution from each of these, they are sufficient to support my bull case. It is increasingly clear that VEEV is able to drive adoption beyond its core products, and it is getting pulled into more systems, more teams, and more workflows across the customer base. My belief is that in such cases, it usually leads to better retention, more cross-sell, and a longer growth runway.

Commercial Solutions is getting stronger

Another reason is that Commercial Solutions should start to see stronger growth ahead. In the recent earnings call, management said there were closer to 140 customers live on Vault CRM, of which two of the top 20 biopharmas are live in major markets, and VEEV ended the year with 10 of the top 20 biopharmas committed globally. The key commentary here is that management expects ~14 of the top 20 to commit over time. While this is pretty much management's own assessment, remember these are large entities that do not make such decisions on short notice. They must have consulted with VEEV for a while, and that gives management a good understanding of whether adoption will come through. In other words, I do think management has good visibility here. The pace of adoption should increase over time as each client becomes a kind of reference base that should make the next wave of decisions easier.

From this, we know that CRM is seeing adoption, but the more encouraging thing here is that CRM is starting to pull other products with it. The commentary that I inferred was that management said some top 20 customers expanded globally with Network and OpenData as they moved to Vault CRM. They also said smaller and mid-sized customers are turning on newer add-ons like Service Center and Campaign Manager. I think this is the more important point. The story is no longer just that VEEV is winning CRM deals. It is that CRM is becoming an entry point for VEEV to cross-sell other commercial solutions. That should increase VEEV’s wallet share and make the customer relationship stickier.

AI

The last point is AI. This has been a real debate point over the past few months as investors worry about agentic AI or clients using AI to replicate whatever VEEV does. My view is that belief is wrong. VEEV is actually a beneficiary of AI. For any agentic AI or AI solutions to work, they need accurate data, and VEEV is the layer that has this proprietary data and validated workflows. This means that VEEV is in the best position to leverage the capabilities of AI.

The product examples we are seeing support my view. For instance, Vault CRM Free Text Agent helps capture compliant call notes. PromoMats agents help approved content move faster. Regulatory and safety agents help to streamline health authority interactions and safety case processing. Management noted that several customers have already gone live with the first VEEV AI agents, and more are underway. These examples matter a lot for the equity story because they suggest this is a product that can work inside existing workflows. Hence, on a like-for-like basis, why would a large client risk adopting a new AI solution (with no access to its data and workflow) vs. adopting VEEV?

Of course, the near-term impact of this will not show up in the headlines, given it is still small, but it does extend the growth runway, as management was clear that it is still early and pricing will start on a token-based model. If one is a believer that AI solutions can improve productivity, overall adoption should increase, and so should token consumption, and that plays into VEEV's pricing strategy.

Valuation

VEEV’s valuation has gotten so cheap that even without any multiples re-rating, I can bridge to a 26% upside. My base view is still that VEEV will see growth accelerate back to 20%. I understand that management's FY27 guidance calls for ~13% y/y revenue growth, but remember that management has a history of underguiding. On average, VEEV beats its revenue guide at ~2.9%, and if we adjust for that, FY27 growth should come closer to ~16%. From there, I think the path to accelerating back to 20% should be well driven by the positive developments I discussed above. VEEV was printing >20% growth just a few years back, so I don’t think this is impossible. Margin-wise, I assume it to stay flat at 43%, making the underlying assumption that VEEV will reinvest every excess profit for growth. Per my model, VEEV should deliver $2.26 billion of adj. EBITDA in FY28. Multiply that by 13x forward EBITDA, which is the all-time low; the stock is still worth ~$220.

Investment Risk

As my base view now leans more on newer products in R&D and Quality gaining real scale, if RTSM, Safety, QMS, LIMS, and other newer modules do not contribute to more revenue growth, the stock narrative could get hurt as investors reset expectations on growth. I would also watch how VEEV’s AI strategy progresses. Management’s positioning is strong, but if AI adoption stays weak and monetization takes longer, this aspect of the equity story may not show up in the valuation anytime soon.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on VEEV. The key update to my thesis is that the growth story now has more legs. R&D and Quality are seeing good adoption; Commercial Solutions are seeing a better cross-sell engine as Vault CRM adoption rises; and AI should strengthen VEEV’s competitive position. The stock also does not need a big rerating for the upside to work.

data.title: UnitedHealth: The CMS Boost Changes Everything Now
data.description:
UNH Is Finally Moving

When UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) heavily sold off in mid-May 2025 on potential DOJ probes and guidance cuts, I initiated my bullish coverage on it, arguing that the sell-off was overblown for the largest publicly traded managed care company in the United States. Since then, the stock hasn't moved much - it recovered by about 15% after the initial meltdown but then went into a prolonged consolidation within the new sideways range of 240-370/share.

Multiple times throughout the past year, I called for an eventual rise and repricing of UNH once the updated management team's efforts start to bear fruit, and now it seems we finally have major signs of that happening. The stock is trending right now before the market's opening (+7.69%) as the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services announced that the average Medicare Advantage payments will be boosted by 2.48% in 2027.

I believe the repricing that's currently taking place is fully justified, and it should keep going throughout calendar year 2026 as the internal fixes at UNH start to impact its finances as well. I still believe that for the position that UNH has on its addressable market, the stock's valuation is too discounted, even considering all the threats/fears. Rating UNH a "Buy" on this news seems to be fair, in my opinion, given the existing upside potential.

Why Do I Think So?

The whole business model of UNH and its peers is too reliant on MCR (Medical Care Ratio), and it's low-margin in nature, so when we saw FY2025 revenues of $447.6 billion (+12% YoY), the firm's adjusted MCR of 88.9%, which was a major 340 bps deterioration from 2024, led to a margin compression that didn't help UNH's price action momentum at all.

The guidance was ugly as well - the management projected a YoY revenue decline of ~2% in FY2026 (~$440 billion at the midpoint) - with the CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) initially proposing a meager 0.09% rate increase for Medicare Advantage in FY2027, the market wasn't excited at all from what we've seen.

But now, the narrative has changed, finally for the bulls.

With medical cost inflation running at ~10%, based on REBA's data, the initial 0.09% rate increase by CMS promised a near-catastrophic margin squeeze for UNH, which is exactly why UNH stock plummeted 20% on the day of the proposal. The finalized 2.48% boost looks like a completely different setup now - it's going to inject >$13 billion into the Medicare Advantage system for 2027, and it's clearly bullish for UnitedHealth, and it's supporting its profitability math ahead.

The updated management has taken an aggressive repricing strategy for 2026, willingly taking the pain for this year by slashing 1.4 million MA members and intentionally driving their medical care ratio down to a targeted 88.8%. The bet was all about building a more profitable baseline, and now, with that 2.48% top-line reimbursement increase applied to their newly built pool, UNH is likely to enjoy a steady margin expansion in FY2027. The FY2026 lower base should help them compare well and show above-consensus YoY earnings growth in FY2027, in my understanding. The news that's currently driving up the stock price as I'm writing these lines is basically removing the existential threat of flat funding risk, so I believe it's just the beginning of a new repricing cycle for UNH for the rest of this year.

After severe earnings revisions to the downside that followed FY2025 quarterly results and all the negative updates throughout the year, we see that FY2027-2029 earnings figures have been ultimately set quite low, and they haven't been repriced to the upside just yet.

In the coming days, I expect multiple analysts to update their financial models with the fresh CMS data in mind, with the most likely results being positive updates to their price targets - all this should finally change the narrative for UNH.

Another probably underappreciated factor in UNH's turnaround story is the management's investments in AI optimizations. They have committed $1.5 billion to AI CAPEX for 2026, with another $1.5 billion planned for FY2027. They plan to extract $1 billion in OPEX this year, driving the operating cost ratio down to 12.8%. As I noted at the very beginning, the whole business model is low-margin in nature, so even a small efficiency gain can drive up EPS at a much stronger pace than analysts price in. The question is execution, but I like the execution level of the new management team so far. For example, the management promised to exit distracting international markets to focus entirely on the U.S. health system, and they did it by selling their last South American asset in late 2025. Another example is Optum Health - the new team promised to rationalize it, and they've already narrowed the affiliated provider network by nearly 20%.

UNH currently uses a generative AI companion named Avery that is being deployed to 6.5 million commercial members and 160,000 MA members, as of the latest update. The management sees it scaling to 20.5 million members by the end of 2026, and if we extrapolate this to FY2027-2028, I guess Avery's contribution to the bottom line should become massively positive.

I mentioned Optum Health above - this business segment's margins collapsed to just 1.5% at the end of 2025, which is too low even in this low-margin environment. But I see it gaining back margins to its historic 6-8% in the next couple of years, as the shedding of unprofitable provider contracts and the consolidation of electronic medical records are realized.

If I'm right at least partially in the above points, the operating leverage should kick in by the end of 2026 and let UNH regain its previous margins within the next couple of years. With higher margins usually come higher valuation multiples.

According to Seeking Alpha, UNH's 5-year median price-to-earnings multiple is 20.35x, while the forward one is only 15.53x - the undervaluation is close to 24% if we use this metric alone. YCharts data shows a 5-year average of 25.19x, so the undervaluation to the historical norm is even larger.

I don't assume that UNH will come back to its average 25x earnings multiple following all the negatives of 2025, but even if it adjusts to 20x, and we see a new consensus FY2027 EPS of $21.05 (5% premium added), the stock has an upside potential of over 40%, even taking into account the post-market move up.

Consensus FY2027 EPS * Premium * Exit P/E = Price target

$20.05 * 1.05 * 20x = $421/share (40%+ upside)

Wall Street sees more modest upside potential of 27.25%, but it still looks solid to me because it's about a third above the current stock price.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

There's still a major risk overhang in the face of the DOJ investigation. The Department of Justice is currently conducting criminal and civil probes into Optum and its potentially systematic inflation of patient risk scores during in-home assessments to overbill the government, according to Bloomberg. There's a risk that the UNH might face a large penalty that will make the next year another down year for the business.

The repricing that I'm assuming as my core base assumption might take too long, or it might not be as aggressively bullish as I'm expecting. For the upside potential that I see to materialize, a lot of stars would have to align next year. UNH's execution, although I like the new management's efforts so far, may not play out as anticipated. At 12-15x forward earnings and no premium on top of the forward EPS, UNH won't move much (it'll actually drop further from here).

The Bottom Line

Despite the clear risks, which I don't think should be ignored, UNH's setup for the rest of this year looks much better with the most recent CMS news, in my view. The cost-cutting and margin-focused initiatives that the new management has been taking should be supported by higher MA payments now in 2027. I expect an eventual continuation of the upside repricing taking place as I'm writing this. With a 40%+ upside potential in mind (for 12-18 months ahead), I confirm my previously given "Buy" rating for UnitedHealth.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Insurers Pop On Medicare Rate Hike
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Insurance stocks jump on Medicare Advantage rate boost: Humana (HUM), UnitedHealth (UNH), lead gains. (00:14) SpaceX (SPACE) details IPO schedule; retail demand expected to break records - report. (01:00) Amazon (AMZN) reduces its reliance on USPS as expected. (02:33)

This is an abridged transcript.

The Trump administration will increase average Medicare Advantage payments by 2.48%, or more than $13 billion, in 2027, according to Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS).

Shares of major insurance companies are higher in premarket action, with Elevance Health (ELV) up 5.3%, UnitedHealth Group (UNH) up 6.9%, Humana (HUM) rising 10.5%, and CVS Health (CVS) up 6.3%.

The Trump administration in January proposed a payment rate hike of 0.09%, which pummeled shares of insurers that run those plans. The payment rate determines how much insurers can charge for monthly premiums and plan benefits they offer and, ultimately, their profits.

SpaceX (SPACE) provided details of its IPO during a Monday night meeting with its bankers.

Reuters reported, citing sources familiar with the matter that SpaceX (SPACE) plans to kick off its IPO roadshow the week of June 8, where company representatives and bankers will pitch to investors.

SpaceX (SPACE) has scheduled a meeting with around 125 financial analysts from the 21 banks involved in the deal the day before.

One of SpaceX’s (SPACE) lead underwriters told the group of 21 investment banks that the level of retail demand and the size of the retail allocation would be something they have “never seen before.”

On June 11, SpaceX (SPACE) plans to host 1,500 retail investors at what the sources described as a major investor event. Alongside U.S. investors, retail investors from the UK, EU, Australia, Canada, Japan, and Korea will also have the opportunity to participate in the offering, the report added.

The IPO prospectus will be made ​public in late May.

Further details like the structure of the deal and precise amount of the retail allocation are ​expected to be finalized closer to ​the IPO launch.

Previous reports indicated that up to 30% of SpaceX’s (SPACE) shares will be allocated to retail, far above the typical 5% to 10% seen in most IPOs. Recently, the space tech company (SPACE) raised its IPO target valuation to more than $2T.

Amazon (AMZN) has a ‌new agreement with the U.S. Postal Service ‌on package deliveries.

USPS will retain about 80% of Amazon’s existing deliveries, which means it will still handle about one billion packages ‌per year.

The reduction was anticipated, but it is not as much as was first reported.

Last month, reports suggested that Amazon would cut at least two thirds of USPS deliveries.

Amazon (AMZN) is the postal services largest customer.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Anthropic targets $30B revenue, signs TPU deal with Google and Broadcom

The stock market has trigged its first ‘proper buy signal’ since Liberation Day – HSBC’s Kettner

Goldman warns copper faces downside risk if Hormuz disruption persists

Stock index futures are lower as President Trump’s deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz approaches.

Trump has given Iran until 8 p.m. Eastern time today to reopen the Strait, warning that failure to comply could trigger U.S. attacks on key infrastructure across the country.

Crude oil is down 0.6% at $111. Bitcoin is up 0.4% at $69,000. Gold is up 0.6% at $4,677.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.3% and the DAX is up 1%. The markets in Hong Kong closed for a holiday.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Shoe Carnival (SCVL) +5% – Shares gained after the CFO purchased 31,000 shares at $16.13 each, representing an investment of roughly $500K, signaling insider confidence in the company’s outlook.

data.title: Calamos Global Convertible Fund: Positioned For Curveballs
data.description:
By Eli Pars, CFA

For Q1 2026, Global convertibles once again performed well compared to equities, delivering a 2.0% gain as measured by the FTSE Global Convertible Bond Index, while the MSCI World Index was down -3.5%. The equities underlying the global convertible index also held up better than the global equity market, declining only -0.84%.

Calamos Global Convertible Fund (CXGCX) moved around with the market during Q1 2026 but finished basically flat, posting a return of 0.50% at quarter end. This is sometimes referred to on Wall Street as “aggressively unchanged.”

As we have discussed in our past commentaries, our goal is lower-volatility participation in the global equity market over the long term. We position the portfolio with the aim of capturing the majority of equity upside while maintaining downside resilience.

We believe our dual focus on achieving returns and mitigating risk will serve the fund. Last quarter, we talked about how “geopolitics can add plenty of curveballs.” Well, that ended up being quite an understatement. But the market is holding up well, in our view. From the start of the Middle East war on February 28 through the end of the quarter, the S&P 500 Index is down just under 5%. On January 1, 2026, the S&P 500 was up 86% cumulatively over the past three years. Considering that, a 5% correction seems pretty tame to us.

We see many of the same tailwinds supporting economic growth as we saw at the start of the year: short-term rates more likely to move lower than higher and a global construction boom driven by the AI data center buildout. However, until we get some resolution in Iran, it is difficult to get aggressive. We’ll continue to lean into convertibles’ downside risk mitigation.

One of the challenges in the global convertible market today is the combination of its narrowness and the violent upside movement of some of the winners, which have risen 5x to 10x within a couple of years. This handful of names is dominated by tech but also includes what we consider a rare creature: a fast-growing European conglomerate. We’ve taken a deliberate approach that seeks to participate in the hottest growth industries without sacrificing the risk mitigation that convertible structures ideally can offer. However, regardless of sector, it’s tough to own converts when they are deep in the money (call it a winner’s curse). But as the stocks continue to work, it’s also tough not owning those converts. Managing position sizes is a balancing act.

Positioning

The fund’s largest allocation continues to be to technology issues. Technology is an area that offers excellent opportunities for our active approach. The universe of convertible tech issuers includes many high-growth opportunities, including AI beneficiaries, and the convertible structure helps dampen volatility. Given the appreciation we’ve seen in many of these names and the increased equity sensitivity, we are closely managing position sizes in this area.

From a regional standpoint, we continue to find the most opportunities in the United States. The fund maintains its underweight to Europe based on the macro environment and bottom-up considerations. As we have discussed, we see a tax and regulatory environment in the region that creates headwinds to company growth. Additionally, Europe’s investor base tends to favor defensive structures, while we prefer securities with a better balance between upside opportunity and downside risk management. That said, we have found opportunities to invest in synthetic convertibles that offer the risk-management and reward characteristics we seek.

Global Convertible Issuance: Hot out of the Gate in 2026

Global convertible issuance for the first quarter is off to a strong start, with $53.3 billion coming to market—more than twice as much as the first quarter of 2025. We saw the strongest issuance in February, and although geopolitics dampened issuance in March, activity was still very strong. For the quarter, US companies led with $27.0 billion, followed by Asia ($14.5 billion) and Europe ($7.6 billion). Japan rounded out the list at $4.2 billion, which is more than double its total issuance for all of 2025.

We see a positive setup for issuance from here, but the bar is high to match the run rate seen in Q2–Q4 of 2025. It’s also important to remember that issuance ebbs and flows due to a variety of factors. Historically, the first month of a quarter tends to see more subdued issuance because of earnings announcements.

Convertible securities vary in their levels of equity and fixed income sensitivity. The most equity-sensitive convertibles offer higher levels of upside but also greater exposure to downside. In contrast, more pronounced fixed income characteristics provide a greater cushion against downside in exchange for less upside opportunity.

The Boom in New Global Convertible Issuance Continued in Q1 2026

Total Return % as of 3/31/26 1Q26 1 Year 3 Year 5 Year 10 Year Fund Inception
Calamos Global Convertible Fund (CXGCX) 0.50 17.02 12.58 3.02 8.05 6.93 (12/31/14)
FTSE Global Convertibles Index 2.00 24.56 13.98 4.72 8.42 7.25

Source: Morningstar. Performance data quoted represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Please refer to important Risk Information. The principal value and return of an investment will fluctuate so that your shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. The funds’ gross expense ratio as of the prospectus dated 2/27/2026 is 1.08% for Class I Shares.

Original Post

data.title: OXLC Is A Mess - Here's Why I'm Bullish
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Oxford Lane (OXLC) shares are up 20% in recent weeks after finding some support near the $8/share level. The stock price experienced steep declines in the past three months after write-offs, and the weakening portfolio hit its NAV/share.

In the previous article, I highlighted a potential market overreaction as the price discount to NAV surged. However, this opportunity should be viewed within the context of elevated risk. Although buying OXLC at a discount to NAV improves the chances of achieving positive total returns, those chances aren't particularly high to begin with. A significant portion of investors sit at a loss despite holding OXLC for many years. A better alternative is the company's notes and preferreds, such as OXLCG, the main focus of this piece.

Capital Losses

OXLC suffers from structural issues that make NAV/share declines inevitable. The company is required to distribute nearly all its ordinary income (interest and dividend income from its CLO portfolio). It is required to make these distributions regardless of capital gains/losses, which have their own distribution test. In theory, capital losses would be offset by capital gains. In reality, it's mostly losses. The reasons a leveraged SME is likely to experience a credit deterioration instead of a credit upgrade are beyond the scope of this piece. For the purpose of this article, it is enough to note that this has historically been the case for OXLC.

Since 2015, the company has written off roughly $900 million in assets at a rate of $75 million annually, a reflection of its ice-cube business model that emphasizes total returns as opposed to capital preservation.

OXLC's business model shifts the credit risk of its loan portfolio to its shareholders instead of managing that risk internally. The monthly dividend decisions are based on cash income, with capital losses (write-offs, defaults within CLO, etc.) treated as an operational issue. The business model is shaped by tax laws as much as economic considerations. As a registered investment company (RIC), OXLC enjoys enormous tax benefits. At the same time, it is required to distribute nearly all its ordinary income, leaving almost nothing to build a loan loss reserve when things go south.

Net Income and Profitability

Two conditions are necessary to successfully invest in subprime loans and derivatives

  • Tolerance for risk
  • Adequate yield to compensate for losses

For OXLC, the question is whether the CLOs generate enough yield to compensate for

  1. Capital Losses
  2. Operating Costs
  3. Financing Costs

One metric that captures all these factors is the "Net Increase in Net Assets Resulting from Operations." It is the equivalent of the "net income".

Using it to gauge the profitability of the core business yields mixed results. Since 2015, "net income" has been inconsistent, with years of steep losses followed by gains. On average, "Net Income" is modest, standing at about $25 million annually.

Most critically, it might not reflect the true performance. OXLC continuously raises equity capital. CLO performance is typically strongest in earlier days, with deterioration being gradual as time passes. Raising capital does artificially boost performance. More importantly, it increases dilution. Although management emphasizes looking at cash income, which it says is the basis of its dividend distribution decisions, it still reports NAV declines.

The Odds: What Holding OXLC for 1 Year Is Like

Investors buying OXLC's common shares are betting that the dividend income will offset the inevitable NAV/share decline by a margin enough to leave a decent yield. Historically, the odds of this happening aren't particularly great. The following analysis assumes an investor buys and holds OXLC for one year.

In this scenario, there is no need to annualize the return because it is a 1-year rolling total return calculation.

The chart below shows that holding OXLC for one year isn't a guarantee for positive returns.

Technical note: The chart excludes holding periods of less than one year. Thus, the cut-off date is January 1st, 2025.

The median return is 6%, the average return is 8%, and the standard deviation is 33%. This means that a typical investor holding OXLC common shares will earn less than an investor holding its fixed-income securities, such as OXLCG.

It is worth noting that the 75th percentile of the observed 1-year rolling returns is ~20%. This means that 25% of observations had a return above 20%. This chance to lock in extraordinary returns contributes to supporting the business model, which depends on continuous capital raises.

OXLGC

Oxford Lane 7.95% Notes due 2031 offer a better risk/reward alternative to holding the common shares.

The chart above shows that the common shares failed to generate annual returns above its 2031 Notes more than half of the time. Only 47% of the observations have a yield above 8%, which is what OXLCG currently yields. OXLC common shareholders aren't being properly compensated for the risk.

Moreover, OXLCG offers a clean exit, with notes maturing in 2031, assuaging the NAV/share decline dynamics impacting the common shareholders. OXLCG now trades below redemption price for the first time in many months, as liquidity evaporates and the risk-off trade intensifies on the back of escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.

Finally, OXLC isn't highly leveraged, with $2.3 billion in assets and $802 billion in liabilities as of December 2025 (p.S-20). In H1'26, interest expense stood at $27 million (or $54 million annualized) (p.31). This is a small figure for a portfolio of high-yield assets measured in billions. For perspective, OXLC generated $109 million in net core NII in Q3'26 alone (three months ended December 2025).

Final Thoughts and Risks

OXLC might not be leveraged on the corporate level, but its CLOs - the underlying portfolio companies - are leveraged SMEs. There are signs of stress in the private equity market, which could have spillover effects on OXLC's underlying CLO loans. A lack of liquidity might have a very detrimental effect on OXLC's common shares. This article carries a speculative buy rating on OXLC common shares because the market is already pricing a significant discount to NAV. The February investor update puts NAV/share between $11.59/share and $11.89/share, a relatively steep discount to the current price of $9.86/share.

The NAV/share declines are part of OXLC's business model. The company determines distributions more based on cash proceeds instead of GAAP "Net Income". The mandatory distribution requirements mean that the company can't build a loan loss reserve. Investors' bet is that the monthly distributions would offset an inevitable decline in NAV/share and share price. Based on the past 1-year rolling returns, the odds of investors earning high total returns aren't particularly great.

The better alternative is OXLC's public notes and preferreds. This article focuses on OXLCG, the company's senior notes maturing in 2031. They offer a smaller but more consistent yield. The notes now trade below redemption price for the first time in many months and currently have a floating yield of 8%. As long as OXLC has access to capital markets, I believe that the model is sustainable.

data.title: U.S. Teens See Gains As Total U.S. Jobs Declines In March 2026
data.description:
According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics' latest jobs data, the number of employed Americans fell by a seasonally-adjusted 64,000 since February, dropping to 162,848,000 in March 2026.

Normally, that situation wouldn't bode well for U.S. teens, but the same employment situation report indicates the number of Age 16 to 19-year-olds counted as having jobs rose by 68,000 from February to March.

Both younger teens (Age 16-17) and older teens (Age 18-19) saw gains. The number of younger teens increased by 37,000 to a seasonally-adjusted 2,000,000 and the number of working older teens grew by 41,000 to 3,459,000.

The following pair of charts presents the seasonally-adjusted data for both the number of teens employed in each of these age categories and also the U.S. teen employment-to-population ratio for the period from January 2021 through March 2026.

Since the total number of seasonally-adjusted jobs fell while the number of working teens increased, that means the number of Americans Age 20 or older had to fall. Which it did, dropping 132,000 to a seasonally-adjusted 157,423,000 in March 2026.

Note that for this jobs data, each demographic gets its own seasonal adjustment, which means this data won't necessarily add up to the seasonally adjusted total for the combined population. If you want numbers that do add up, you'll want to extract the non-seasonally adjusted data from the BLS' labor force statistics database.

Overall, the March 2026 employment situation data is a little strange. Perhaps its strangeness is attributable to run-of-the-mill sampling variation, but we'll know if there's something more behind it over the next few months.

References

U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Labor Force Statistics (Current Population Survey - CPS). [Online Database]. Accessed: 2 April 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bowman Consulting: Capturing Utility Growth Without Paying A Premium
data.description:
In my last article, I wanted to explain the global electrification boom in recent years. The article was about American Electric Power (AEP), which seemed to be a very interesting option to participate in the mentioned boom, thanks to its powerful energy infrastructure based on coal and large investments in transmission. If you have some stock in AEP, I recommend keeping it. But I wouldn't add to my position, at least for now.

AEP has a market cap of $71 billion, so it is a high-quality option. These companies are very analyzed by the market that has already priced in their potential. That's why, sometimes, the best opportunities are in companies of small and mid capitalization. In this case, I see that opportunity in Bowman Consulting (BWMN), an energy infrastructure construction company that is getting positioned in the electrification boom.

With a market cap of $470 million, the company doesn't appear to have been viewed by the market as an attractive investment in Utilities (XLU). And that's because BWMN operates in four segments, each one with significant internal diversification. In the same way it builds streets, houses, and offices, it also provides full-service solutions for power generation and data centers. Its low market cap also has some risks, such as a higher sensitivity to interest rates, but I believe the cost-benefit analysis is favorable. Besides, the company is trading below its peers while its backlog continues to grow.

The electrification boom

I want to go back just for a moment to the electrification boom. I believe the Middle East conflict will, once again, demonstrate the need for energy diversification. Yes, it's not only an energy transition. I do believe the world needs more diversification. I would even say that greater energy diversification accelerates the transition to renewable sources.

On the way to transition, I believe that to diversify is also to use non-renewable sources. It's not only oil but also coal. It also means accepting that nuclear energy is not only useful but safe. Even more: Analysts estimate nuclear energy will go from 33 TWh to 65 TWh by 2030. The world will, indeed, need more energy. Maybe more than ever before. The middle class is growing, and new technologies need more electricity: EVs and charging stations, heating replacements, consumer electronics, and AI data centers. Until 2030, electricity demand will grow 3.6% annually, according to the IEA.

Industry and the residential sector will continue to be the big drivers of demand, with strong growth in data centers. This goes in line with the uses I mentioned before: electricity replacing gas in both industries and buildings, and AI revolutionizing the world of data centers.

This electrification boom will need very strong investments in energy infrastructure, besides generation. Investment in electricity networks is estimated to increase from $390 billion in 2024 to approximately $600 billion in 2030. The largest expenditures require infrastructure improvements. For example, the expansion of the network to reach places that were not previously thought to be reachable, the use of intelligent systems to maintain affordability, or making the transmission system stronger to withstand higher voltage.

The barrier to entry in a costly business led me to Bowman Consulting

Besides generation sources, the infrastructure that will sustain energy is what I like to invest in. And here comes Bowman Consulting. Generators typically also participate in transmission (where electricity travels at its highest voltage) and in distribution (where lower loads travel to reach the residential sector).

I believe this is something logical, because its operations are yet so robust that they could offer better services than a newly arrived player. For example, American Electric Power has planned an investment of $72 billion through 2030, with assets already valued at $114 billion (slide 111 of 10-K). Entering into the electrification business (generation, transmission, and distribution, mainly) is too expensive.

However, the engineering, design, and logistics of network construction, along with data center services, are a more specialized job. Bowman does that job and is increasing its exposure to utilities more and more. And utilities are very important because they are the sector that contributes most to energy resilience. Behind them are the industrial companies that are dedicated to the manufacture of equipment and related constructions.

Utility exposure in Bowman's portfolio

It is in this context that I'm paying attention to Bowman. Its backlog in utilities increased from 13% in 2022 to 24% in 2025. In the following chart, you can see the bigger weight in the portfolio, marked in yellow.

I believe the decrease in weight in building and transport infrastructure is clear. I also see good performance in natural resources, with a significant weight in the advance of mining and infrastructure services in water systems.

With its backlog declining, Building Infrastructure is facing a weak real estate market, with low demand and low affordability in multi-family housing. Mortgage rates above 6% for 30-year loans do not help the segment. While industrial offices are facing another low demand crisis since the pandemic. In this case, the increase of home offices was a very important downward catalyst. Building infrastructure still has a strong weight in revenues, representing 42% of the total. It is also the segment with the best gross margin: 55.8%.

But as compensation for the weaker momentum in futures contracts, the greater exposure to utilities offers significant opportunities. The backlog is just a sample of that. The power, utilities, and energy sector represented 23% of income in 2025, while its gross margin was 55.3%, just 50 bps below Building Infrastructure.

Within the revenues from utilities, traditional energy represents 14%, being the most significant. Data centers, at the closing of Q4 2025, represented 6%, when two quarters ago it was 4%. For Bowman, the data centers business offers a lot of possibilities, and I am waiting for it to keep growing in the following quarters. McKinsey estimates an investment necessary in data centers of $1.3 trillion through 2030. For a company with such a small capitalization, it represents a significant market opportunity. Besides, Bowman takes advantage of the utilization of several services thanks to its logistical knowledge. The multi-service advantage was something I already suspected when researching the company. But I found a better explanation given by the COO of the company, Daniel Swayze:

Data center programs are rarely single service projects. They are multiphase, multiservice opportunities. For example, combining the electrical and mechanical engineering forces from our e3i acquisition, the fire and life safety design services from our Fisher acquisition with our established capabilities in civil planning and engineering, we have a strong service offering our clients can rely on.

Conference call

Q4 2025: Revenues confirm a growing business

Before seeing the public services catalyst and the electrification boom (along with the demand for AI data centers), Bowman is already showing a business that delivers very good results. And I believe one of the main characteristics is the diversification of sectors in each segment. This means that no single segment has too much weight on revenue. And when one gets weaker (such as building infrastructure in the last years), others grow, such as natural resources and utilities.

That combination of sectors shows an incredible revenue increase from $261 million in 2022 to $420 million in 2025. Revenues for Q1 2026 are expected to be between $496 and $510 million. But it's not only revenues, because margins play a key role in companies that offer infrastructure services. And margins are growing too. Adjusted EBITDA margin went from 14.2% in 2022 to 16.8% in 2025. For 2026, the company estimates an adjusted EBITDA margin between 17% and 17.5%.

The $490 million for 2025 exceeded the upper range of their estimate by $10 million. However, both earnings per share and revenues of Q4 didn't surpass the consensus estimate. GAAP EPS fell short of estimates by $0.13, while revenues were $152k below expectations. I believe the market sometimes over-punishes some quarterly misses, and that was part of the drop in the price of its shares in recent months. But in 2025, looking at the full picture, I believe it was a very good year.

Finally, what I see in a company with a low market cap is its cash generation. Even more so when it is dedicated to a sector that uses a lot of capital, such as infrastructure and logistics. The FCF conversion was 46% in 2025 versus 40% in 2024. I mean, from the $73 million of adjusted EBITDA, $33.4 million was cash generation. Added to that is net debt of $178 million and a leverage ratio of 2.45x, which they expect to lower to 2x in 2026. They also have $154 million available from a line of credit. In summary, I see a solid financial position in a sector with high capital intensity and, most importantly, strong cash generation during these uncertain times with high interest rates.

Attractive valuation in an expensive sector

As I said at the beginning, Bowman is trading at some discounts due to the recent drop in its share price. In the last six months, it fell more than 30%, while so far this year, it has dropped 15%. Given its past strong growth and continued solid growth prospects, PEG FWD of 0.9x is almost 40% below the industry median.

Furthermore, expected EV/EBITDA is 8.3x versus the industry median of 11.4x, a difference of approximately 27%. To make a comparison with other similar companies, I looked at other metrics of Orion Group Holdings (ORN), which has a similar market cap. ORN has an EV/EBITDA FWD of 8.9x but trades at 181x its earnings per share, while BWMN trades at 39x. Another similar company is Concrete Pumping (BBCP), and its P/E is 73x. BWMN may appear expensive on a P/E basis, but compared to its peers, it trades at a reasonable valuation. Besides, as I've said before, the exposure of BWMN to utilities could cause an expensive valuation. But that isn't happening. If I compare it with Quanta Services (PWR), the biggest company in the sector, the difference gets even bigger. PWR has an EV/EBITDA FWD of 25x and trades at 80 times its earnings.

In summary, Bowman still has cheap prices in a sector like utilities that is usually expensive. Its exposure to utilities today is about a quarter of its operations, but it could be more in the future. If I consider only the difference between the EV/EBITDA of 8.3x versus the sector average of 11.4x, the undervaluation is 27%. Applied to the current price, I estimate a target price between $34 and $37 based on the growth potential for 2026.

Risks

Part of the risk of investing in Bowman is that its capacity might not be enough to supply the demand of the electrification boom. For the bigger companies of the sector, the risk, I believe, is not being able to quickly monetize the investment in the mid-term, although in some cases it far exceeds its market cap. Bowman is protected from that risk but is exposed to another: the challenge of scaling its capacity fast enough to meet growing demand.

Bowman has no responsibility in generation, transmission, or distribution, but it could lose ground in a highly competitive market. For example, Quanta Services (PWR) has a higher exposure to electric infrastructure due to its bigger size (market cap: $82 billion). I don't think it's a fair comparison with Bowman because they are different services, but it can compete for the same customers in the power, utilities, and energy segment. Despite those risks, Bowman has an advantage due to, precisely, its diversification, and that's why it is a very attractive investment for those looking for a more moderate exposure to the electrification boom. My thesis is that exposure will increase, and it will be reflected in the share price.

Conclusions

The electrification boom, catalyzed by AI data centers, I believe represents a long-term investment opportunity. Unfortunately, I believe the market got ahead of that boom in 2025 and priced at a premium valuation for the utilities sector. But Bowman is a hidden infrastructure company that seems to have gone unnoticed.

Its financial strength, revenue growth, and growing backlog are driving the company's increasing exposure to utilities. I believe the market hasn't priced in the fact that Bowman can become a key part of the chain value of AI data centers. While hyperscalers invest billions in data centers, the big generators and ex-crypto miners are gaining prominence. Behind those giants appears Bowman, which is trading at attractive valuations, representing a unique opportunity in a typically expensive sector.
